does: This paper develops an interpretable machine learning model that identifies moments of mechanistic reasoning in student team conversations by outputting time-varying probabilities of individual students engaging in such reasoning. The model processes both individual utterances and group contributions, using probabilistic models with specially-designed inductive biases that encode domain knowledge about mechanistic reasoning. The authors systematically compare models with and without these inductive bias components through experiments on transcripts from unseen students and novel discussion contexts, demonstrating that incorporating domain-aligned inductive biases improves the model's ability to generalize and locate mechanistic reasoning acts.
connects: This work bridges educational research and machine learning by addressing a practical bottleneck in STEM education research—manually searching transcripts for pedagogically significant moments. It builds on prior work in educational data mining and dialogue analysis that seeks to automatically identify learning behaviors in naturalistic conversations. The paper integrates interpretable machine learning approaches with probabilistic graphical models, extending methods from computational linguistics and sequence modeling that leverage contextual information. The emphasis on inductive bias to encode domain knowledge connects to broader trends in machine learning that combine learned representations with structured prior knowledge, moving away from purely data-driven approaches toward more interpretable, domain-informed models.
enables: This approach opens pathways for scaling analyses of collaborative learning across large corpora of team conversations, allowing educators to efficiently discover high-value moments for deeper qualitative study. The methodology could extend to identifying other forms of scientific reasoning or learning behaviors in educational contexts, providing a template for automating detection of nuanced cognitive processes. The framework of using interpretable probabilistic models with inductive bias could inform development of real-time feedback systems for students or instructors during collaborative work. More broadly, it suggests how domain expertise can be incorporated into machine learning pipelines to improve both performance and trustworthiness, with potential applications beyond education to other fields requiring human behavior analysis in specialized contexts.
